1: A direct type: 'When anyone brings a grain offering [minchah] as an offering to YHWH, his offering shall be of fine flour. He shall pour oil on it and put frankincense on it.' The grain offering's three elements are a three-way type: fine flour (solet) = Christ's sinless humanity — the finest grain, sifted without blemish; oil = the Holy Spirit — Jesus was anointed with the Spirit (Acts 10:38; Luke 4:18); frankincense = prayer and intercession — 'The prayers of the saints... golden bowls full of incense' (Revelation 5:8). The grain offering is the type of the Incarnation.
2: A shadow: 'And he shall bring it to Aaron's sons the priests. And he shall take from it a handful of the fine flour and oil, with all of its frankincense, and the priest shall burn this as its memorial portion on the altar, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The azkarah (memorial/reminder portion) represents the whole — a handful stands for the entire offering. Christ's one sacrifice represents and effects the whole of atonement: 'He has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Hebrews 9:26).
3: A direct type: 'But the rest of the grain offering shall be for Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy part of YHWH's food offerings.' The priests eat the remainder of the grain offering — they are sustained by the sacrifice. Christ is both the offering and the bread: 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35); 'the bread that I will give for the life of the world is my flesh' (John 6:51). The priests eating the grain offering type the believer's feeding on Christ in faith and in the Eucharist (1 Corinthians 10:16).
4: A direct type: 'When you bring a grain offering baked in the oven as an offering, it shall be unleavened loaves of fine flour mixed with oil, or unleavened wafers smeared with oil.' Unleavened (matzot) = without leaven = without sin. Paul interprets leaven as sin and malice (1 Corinthians 5:7–8): 'Cleanse out the old leaven... as you really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.' The absence of leaven in the grain offering is the type of Christ's sinlessness — the fine flour of his humanity, tested and baked by suffering (Hebrews 2:10), contains no corruption.
5: A shadow: 'And if your offering is a grain offering cooked on a griddle, it shall be of fine flour unleavened, mixed with oil.' The multiple preparation forms (oven, griddle, pan) show that the sacrificial principle applies across varied forms — as does Christ's one sacrifice: applicable across all times, peoples, and circumstances.
6: A direct type: 'You shall break it in pieces and pour oil on it; it is a grain offering.' Breaking in pieces with oil poured over — a eucharistic image: 'He broke it and gave it to the disciples, saying, Take, eat; this is my body' (Matthew 26:26). The grain offering of broken pieces with oil is the OT form of what Christ instituted at the Last Supper.
7: A shadow: 'And if your offering is a grain offering cooked in a pan, it shall be made of fine flour with oil.' The third preparation method; the consistent requirements — fine flour, oil, no leaven — remain across all forms. Christ's nature is constant across all expressions of his work: the same sinless humanity, the same Spirit-anointing, whether as teacher, healer, or sacrifice.
8: A shadow: 'And you shall bring the grain offering that is made of these things to YHWH, and when it is presented to the priest, he shall bring it to the altar.' The offering must be brought to the priest, who brings it to the altar. Christ is both the offerer who brings himself and the priest who presents the sacrifice — the author and perfecter of our faith who 'offered himself without blemish to God' (Hebrews 9:14).
9: A shadow: 'And the priest shall take from the grain offering its memorial portion and burn this on the altar, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The azkarah burned on the altar is the portion that ascends — the part given wholly to God. In Christ's sacrifice, the offering is entirely God's: 'He offered himself without blemish to God' (Hebrews 9:14). The Father accepted it: 'a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Ephesians 5:2).
10: A shadow: 'But the rest of the grain offering shall be for Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy part of YHWH's food offerings.' What is not burned belongs to the priests — holy food for holy service. The gospel makes believers a 'royal priesthood' (1 Peter 2:9) who feed on Christ. What Christ offers entirely to God, he then gives as food to his priests.
11: A direct type: 'No grain offering that you bring to YHWH shall be made with leaven, for you shall burn no leaven and no honey as a food offering to YHWH.' The absolute prohibition on leaven makes it the most explicit type in the grain offering: leaven = sin and corruption. 'Do you not know that a little leaven leavens the whole lump? Cleanse out the old leaven... for Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (1 Corinthians 5:6–7). The no-leaven rule announces that atonement requires a sinless sacrifice — only Christ qualifies.
12: A shadow: 'As an offering of firstfruits you may bring them to YHWH, but they shall not be offered on the altar for a pleasing aroma.' The graduated system: first-fruits may include leaven and honey but they cannot ascend as atoning sacrifice. Only what is without leaven can atone. This shows that even good things — the natural produce of the earth — cannot serve as an atoning sacrifice. Only the one without leaven can bear sin.
13: A direct type: 'You shall season all your grain offerings with salt. You shall not let the salt of the covenant with your God be lacking from your grain offering; with all your offerings you shall offer salt.' Salt = covenant permanence and incorruption (salt preserves). The 'covenant of salt' (berit melach) is the most permanent of covenants — it cannot be corrupted. Christ's sacrifice establishes 'the eternal covenant' (Hebrews 13:20) by his blood — the incorruptible salt-covenant that never expires.
14: A direct type: 'If you offer a grain offering of firstfruits to YHWH, you shall offer for the grain offering of your firstfruits fresh ears, roasted with fire, crushed new grain.' The first-fruits offering is a type of the resurrection. Paul: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Corinthians 15:20). Jesus is the first-fruits grain — roasted and crushed (death and burial), but the first to rise from the ground in resurrection.
15: A shadow: 'And you shall put oil on it and lay frankincense on it; it is a grain offering.' Oil and frankincense on the first-fruits: the Spirit and prayer/intercession attend the first-fruits offering. At the resurrection, the Spirit who raised Jesus (Romans 8:11) is the same who anointed him. The risen Christ intercedes (Hebrews 7:25) — frankincense rising before God.
16: A shadow: 'And the priest shall burn the memorial portion, some of the crushed grain and some of the oil with all of its frankincense; it is a food offering to YHWH.' The first-fruits' azkarah burns as a pleasing aroma — the culminating promise of the grain offering chapter: even the first-fruits, roasted and crushed, are accepted by God. The resurrection is not a divine afterthought but an accepted offering: 'God... gave him glory, so that your faith and hope are in God' (1 Peter 1:21).
